# Organizing Build Infrastructure with Directory.Build Files

## Directory.Build.props vs Directory.Build.targets

Understanding which file to use is critical. They differ in **when** they are imported during evaluation:

**Evaluation order:**

```
Directory.Build.props → SDK .props → YourProject.csproj → SDK .targets → Directory.Build.targets
```

| Use `.props` for | Use `.targets` for |
|---|---|
| Setting property defaults | Custom build targets |
| Common item definitions | Late-bound property overrides |
| Properties projects can override | Post-build steps |
| Assembly/package metadata | Conditional logic on final values |
| Analyzer PackageReferences | Targets that depend on SDK-defined properties |

**Rule of thumb:** Properties and items go in `.props`. Custom targets and late-bound logic go in `.targets`.

Because `.props` is imported before the project file, the project can override any value set there. Because `.targets` is imported after everything, it gets the final say—but projects cannot override `.targets` values.

### ⚠️ Critical: TargetFramework Availability in .props vs .targets

**Property conditions on `$(TargetFramework)` in `.props` files silently fail for single-targeting projects** — the property is empty during `.props` evaluation. Move TFM-conditional properties to `.targets` instead. ItemGroup and Target conditions are not affected.

See [targetframework-props-pitfall.md](references/targetframework-props-pitfall.md) for the full explanation.

## Directory.Build.props

Good candidates: language settings, assembly/package metadata, build warnings, code analysis, common analyzers.

```xml
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <Company>Contoso</Company>
    <Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

**Do NOT put here:** project-specific TFMs, project-specific PackageReferences, targets/build logic, or properties depending on SDK-defined values (not available during `.props` evaluation).

## Directory.Build.targets

Good candidates: custom build targets, late-bound property overrides (values depending on SDK properties), post-build validation.

```xml
<Project>
  <Target Name="ValidateProjectSettings" BeforeTargets="Build">
    <Error Text="All libraries must target netstandard2.0 or higher"
           Condition="'$(OutputType)' == 'Library' AND '$(TargetFramework)' == 'net472'" />
  </Target>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <!-- DocumentationFile depends on OutputPath, which is set by the SDK -->
    <DocumentationFile Condition="'$(IsPackable)' == 'true'">$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

## Directory.Packages.props (Central Package Management)

Central Package Management (CPM) provides a single source of truth for all NuGet package versions. See [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/nuget/consume-packages/central-package-management](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/nuget/consume-packages/central-package-management) for details.

**Enable CPM in `Directory.Packages.props` at the repo root:**

```xml
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging" Version="8.0.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.9.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.2" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <!-- GlobalPackageReference applies to ALL projects — great for analyzers -->
    <GlobalPackageReference Include="StyleCop.Analyzers" Version="1.2.0-beta.556" />
    <GlobalPackageReference Include="Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.NetAnalyzers" Version="8.0.0" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>
```

## Directory.Build.rsp

Contains default MSBuild CLI arguments applied to all builds under the directory tree.

**Example `Directory.Build.rsp`:**

```
/maxcpucount
/nodeReuse:false
/consoleLoggerParameters:Summary;ForceNoAlign
/warnAsMessage:MSB3277
```

- Works with both `msbuild` and `dotnet` CLI in modern .NET versions
- Great for enforcing consistent CI and local build flags
- Each argument goes on its own line

## Multi-level Directory.Build Files

MSBuild only auto-imports the **first** `Directory.Build.props` (or `.targets`) it finds walking up from the project directory. To chain multiple levels, explicitly import the parent at the **top** of the inner file. See [multi-level-examples](references/multi-level-examples.md) for full file examples.

```xml
<Project>
  <Import Project="$([MSBuild]::GetPathOfFileAbove('Directory.Build.props', '$(MSBuildThisFileDirectory)../'))"
         Condition="Exists('$([MSBuild]::GetPathOfFileAbove('Directory.Build.props', '$(MSBuildThisFileDirectory)../'))')" />

  <!-- Inner-level overrides go here -->
</Project>
```

**Example layout:**

```
repo/
  Directory.Build.props          ← repo-wide (lang version, company info, analyzers)
  Directory.Build.targets        ← repo-wide targets
  Directory.Packages.props       ← central package versions
  src/
    Directory.Build.props        ← src-specific (imports repo-level, sets IsPackable=true)
  test/
    Directory.Build.props        ← test-specific (imports repo-level, sets IsPackable=false, adds test packages)
```

## Artifact Output Layout (.NET 8+)

Set `<ArtifactsPath>$(MSBuildThisFileDirectory)artifacts</ArtifactsPath>` in `Directory.Build.props` to automatically produce project-name-separated `bin/`, `obj/`, and `publish/` directories under a single `artifacts/` folder, avoiding bin/obj clashes by default. See [common-patterns](references/common-patterns.md) for the directory layout and additional patterns (conditional settings by project type, post-pack validation).

## Workflow: Organizing Build Infrastructure

1. **Audit all `.csproj` files** — Catalog every `<PropertyGroup>`, `<ItemGroup>`, and custom `<Target>` across the solution. Note which settings repeat and which are project-specific.
2. **Create root `Directory.Build.props`** — Move shared property defaults (LangVersion, Nullable, TreatWarningsAsErrors, metadata) here. These are imported before the project file so projects can override them.
3. **Create root `Directory.Build.targets`** — Move custom build targets, post-build validation, and any properties that depend on SDK-defined values (e.g., `OutputPath`, `TargetFramework` for single-targeting projects) here. These are imported after the SDK so all properties are available.
4. **Create `Directory.Packages.props`** — Enable Central Package Management (`ManagePackageVersionsCentrally`), list all `PackageVersion` entries, and remove `Version=` from `PackageReference` items in `.csproj` files.
5. **Set up multi-level hierarchy** — Create inner `Directory.Build.props` files for `src/` and `test/` folders with distinct settings. Use `GetPathOfFileAbove` to chain to the parent.
6. **Simplify `.csproj` files** — Remove all centralized properties, version attributes, and duplicated targets. Each project should only contain what is unique to it.
7. **Validate** — Run `dotnet restore && dotnet build` and verify no regressions. Use `dotnet msbuild -pp:output.xml` to inspect the final merged view if needed.

## Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `Directory.Build.props` isn't picked up | File name casing wrong (exact match required on Linux/macOS) | Verify exact casing: `Directory.Build.props` (capital D, B) |
| Properties from `.props` are ignored by projects | Project sets the same property after the import | Move the property to `Directory.Build.targets` to set it after the project |
| Multi-level import doesn't work | Missing `GetPathOfFileAbove` import in inner file | Add the `<Import>` element at the top of the inner file (see Multi-level section) |
| Properties using SDK values are empty in `.props` | SDK properties aren't defined yet during `.props` evaluation | Move to `.targets` which is imported after the SDK |
| `Directory.Packages.props` not found | File not at repo root or not named exactly | Must be named `Directory.Packages.props` and at or above the project directory |
| Property condition on `$(TargetFramework)` doesn't match in `.props` | `TargetFramework` isn't set yet for single-targeting projects during `.props` evaluation | Move property to `.targets`, or use ItemGroup/Target conditions instead (which evaluate late) |

**Diagnosis:** Use the preprocessed project output to see all imports and final property values:

```bash
dotnet msbuild -pp:output.xml MyProject.csproj
```

This expands all imports inline so you can see exactly where each property is set and what the final evaluated value is.

| Assembly/package metadata | Conditional logic on final values |
| Analyzer PackageReferences | Targets that depend on SDK-defined properties |

Because `.props` is imported before the project file, the project can override any value set there. Because `.targets` is imported after everything, it gets the final say—but projects cannot override `.targets` values.

**Property conditions on `$(TargetFramework)` in `.props` files silently fail for single-targeting projects** — the property is empty during `.props` evaluation. Move TFM-conditional properties to `.targets` instead. ItemGroup and Target conditions are not affected.

See [targetframework-props-pitfall.md](references/targetframework-props-pitfall.md) for the full explanation.

## Directory.Build.props

Good candidates: language settings, assembly/package metadata, build warnings, code analysis, common analyzers.

```xml
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <Company>Contoso</Company>
    <Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

**Do NOT put here:** project-specific TFMs, project-specific PackageReferences, targets/build logic, or properties depending on SDK-defined values (not available during `.props` evaluation).

## Directory.Build.targets

Good candidates: custom build targets, late-bound property overrides (values depending on SDK properties), post-build validation.

```xml
<Project>
  <Target Name="ValidateProjectSettings" BeforeTargets="Build">
    <Error Text="All libraries must target netstandard2.0 or higher"
           Condition="'$(OutputType)' == 'Library' AND '$(TargetFramework)' == 'net472'" />
  </Target>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <!-- DocumentationFile depends on OutputPath, which is set by the SDK -->
    <DocumentationFile Condition="'$(IsPackable)' == 'true'">$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

```

Central Package Management (CPM) provides a single source of truth for all NuGet package versions. See [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/nuget/consume-packages/central-package-management](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/nuget/consume-packages/central-package-management) for details.

```xml

  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>

    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging" Version="8.0.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.9.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.2" />
  </ItemGroup>

    <!-- GlobalPackageReference applies to ALL projects — great for analyzers -->
    <GlobalPackageReference Include="StyleCop.Analyzers" Version="1.2.0-beta.556" />

Contains default MSBuild CLI arguments applied to all builds under the directory tree.

**Example `Directory.Build.rsp`:**

/nodeReuse:false
/consoleLoggerParameters:Summary;ForceNoAlign
/warnAsMessage:MSB3277
```

- Great for enforcing consistent CI and local build flags
- Each argument goes on its own line

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a clear picture. Let me analyze the issues:

1. **Duplicated properties across all 5 projects:**
   - `TargetFramework`, `Nullable`, `ImplicitUsings`, `TreatWarningsAsErrors`, `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild`, `Company`, `Authors` — repeated in ALL 5 projects

2. **Duplicated targets:**
   - `PrintBuildInfo` target duplicated in all 3 src projects

3. **Duplicated DocumentationFile property:**
   - `DocumentationFile` property using `$(OutputPath)` repeated in all 3 src projects — and this should be in `.targets` since it depends on `OutputPath` (an SDK-defined property)
   
4. **Package version inconsistencies:**
   - No centralized package management, so versions drift across projects — `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions` varies between `8.0.0` and `8.0.1`, and test frameworks have significant version gaps like `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` at `17.10.0` versus `17.9.0`

5. **Test projects need alignment:**
   - Both test projects repeat the same `IsPackable=false` setting and test dependencies, but with mismatched versions across xunit and related packages

6. **Missing build infrastructure:**
   - There's no `Directory.Build.props` or `Directory.Build.targets` file at the root level to centralize common settings

I'm thinking through whether the user wants just recommendations or a full implementation. They asked to "suggest how to improve," which could mean advice, but a working solution would be more valuable. Let me provide a comprehensive plan with the actual changes needed.

I'll create the centralized build files and refactor the project files to eliminate duplication.

1. # Common Directory.Build Patterns
2. 
3. ## Conditional Settings by Project Type
4. 
5. Detect test projects by naming convention in `Directory.Build.props`:
6. 
7. ```xml
8. <PropertyGroup Condition="$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests')) OR $(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.UnitTests'))">
9.   <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
10.   <IsTestProject>true</IsTestProject>
11. </PropertyGroup>
12. ```
13. 
14. Use `Directory.Build.targets` for conditions on SDK-defined properties like `OutputType`:
15. 
16. ```xml
17. <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(OutputType)' == 'Exe'">
18.   <SelfContained>false</SelfContained>
19. </PropertyGroup>
20. 
21. <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(OutputType)' == 'Library' AND '$(IsTestProject)' != 'true'">
22.   <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
23. </PropertyGroup>
24. ```
25. 
26. ## Post-Build Validation
27. 
28. Validate that `Pack` produced the expected output:
29. 
30. ```xml
31. <Target Name="ValidatePackageOutput" AfterTargets="Pack"
32.         Condition="'$(IsPackable)' == 'true'">
33.   <Error Text="Package was not created at $(PackageOutputPath)$(PackageId).$(PackageVersion).nupkg"
34.          Condition="!Exists('$(PackageOutputPath)$(PackageId).$(PackageVersion).nupkg')" />
35. </Target>
36. ```
37. 
38. ## Artifact Output Layout (.NET 8+)
39. 
40. Setting `ArtifactsPath` in `Directory.Build.props` produces this structure:
41. 
42. ```
43. artifacts/
44.   bin/
45.     MyLib/
46.       debug/
47.       release/
48.     MyApp/
49.       debug/
50.       release/
51.   obj/
52.     MyLib/
53.     MyApp/
54.   publish/
55.     MyApp/
56. ```
57.

 # Common Directory.Build Patterns
 
 ## Conditional Settings by Project Type
 
 Detect test projects by naming convention in `Directory.Build.props`:
 
 ```xml
 <PropertyGroup Condition="$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests')) OR $(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.UnitTests'))">
   <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
   <IsTestProject>true</IsTestProject>
 </PropertyGroup>
 ```
 
 Use `Directory.Build.targets` for conditions on SDK-defined properties like `OutputType`:
 
 ```xml
 <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(OutputType)' == 'Exe'">
   <SelfContained>false</SelfContained>
 </PropertyGroup>
 
 <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(OutputType)' == 'Library' AND '$(IsTestProject)' != 'true'">
   <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
 </PropertyGroup>
 ```
 
 ## Post-Build Validation
 
 Validate that `Pack` produced the expected output:
 
 ```xml
 <Target Name="ValidatePackageOutput" AfterTargets="Pack"
         Condition="'$(IsPackable)' == 'true'">
   <Error Text="Package was not created at $(PackageOutputPath)$(PackageId).$(PackageVersion).nupkg"
          Condition="!Exists('$(PackageOutputPath)$(PackageId).$(PackageVersion).nupkg')" />
 </Target>
 ```
 
 ## Artifact Output Layout (.NET 8+)
 
 Setting `ArtifactsPath` in `Directory.Build.props` produces this structure:
 
 ```
 artifacts/
   bin/
     MyLib/
       debug/
       release/
     MyApp/
       debug/
       release/
   obj/
     MyLib/
     MyApp/
   publish/
     MyApp/
 ```

3. ## Conditional Settings by Project Type
4. 
5. Detect test projects by naming convention in `Directory.Build.props`:
6. 
7. ```xml
8. <PropertyGroup Condition="$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests')) OR $(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.UnitTests'))">
9.   <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
10.   <IsTestProject>true</IsTestProject>
11. </PropertyGroup>
12. ```
13. 
14. Use `Directory.Build.targets` for conditions on SDK-defined properties like `OutputType`:
15. 
16. ```xml
17. <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(OutputType)' == 'Exe'">
18.   <SelfContained>false</SelfContained>
19. </PropertyGroup>
20. 
21. <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(OutputType)' == 'Library' AND '$(IsTestProject)' != 'true'">
22.   <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
23. </PropertyGroup>
24. ```
25. 
26. ## Post-Build Validation
27. 
28. Validate that `Pack` produced the expected output:
29. 
30. ```xml
31. <Target Name="ValidatePackageOutput" AfterTargets="Pack"
32.         Condition="'$(IsPackable)' == 'true'">
33.   <Error Text="Package was not created at $(PackageOutputPath)$(PackageId).$(PackageVersion).nupkg"
34.          Condition="!Exists('$(PackageOutputPath)$(PackageId).$(PackageVersion).nupkg')" />
35. </Target>
36. ```
37. 
38. ## Artifact Output Layout (.NET 8+)
39. 
40. Setting `ArtifactsPath` in `Directory.Build.props` produces this structure:
41. 
42. ```
43. artifacts/
44.   bin/
45.     MyLib/
46.       debug/
47.       release/
48.     MyApp/
49.       debug/
50.       release/
51.   obj/
52.     MyLib/
53.     MyApp/
54.   publish/
55.     MyApp/
56. ```
57.

 # Common Directory.Build Patterns
 
 ## Conditional Settings by Project Type
 
 Detect test projects by naming convention in `Directory.Build.props`:
 
 ```xml
 <PropertyGroup Condition="$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests')) OR $(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.UnitTests'))">
   <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
   <IsTestProject>true</IsTestProject>
 </PropertyGroup>
 ```
 
 Use `Directory.Build.targets` for conditions on SDK-defined properties like `OutputType`:
 
 ```xml
 <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(OutputType)' == 'Exe'">
   <SelfContained>false</SelfContained>
 </PropertyGroup>
 
 <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(OutputType)' == 'Library' AND '$(IsTestProject)' != 'true'">
   <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
 </PropertyGroup>
 ```
 
 ## Post-Build Validation
 
 Validate that `Pack` produced the expected output:
 
 ```xml
 <Target Name="ValidatePackageOutput" AfterTargets="Pack"
         Condition="'$(IsPackable)' == 'true'">
   <Error Text="Package was not created at $(PackageOutputPath)$(PackageId).$(PackageVersion).nupkg"
          Condition="!Exists('$(PackageOutputPath)$(PackageId).$(PackageVersion).nupkg')" />
 </Target>
 ```
 
 ## Artifact Output Layout (.NET 8+)
 
 Setting `ArtifactsPath` in `Directory.Build.props` produces this structure:
 
 ```
 artifacts/
   bin/
     MyLib/
       debug/
       release/
     MyApp/
       debug/
       release/
   obj/
     MyLib/
     MyApp/
   publish/
     MyApp/
 ```

I found significant duplication across all 5 projects. Here's what I see:

| Issue | Details |
|-------|---------|
| **6 properties duplicated × 5 projects** | Nullable, ImplicitUsings, TreatWarningsAsErrors, EnforceCodeStyleInBuild, Company, Authors |
| **TargetFramework** | Same `net8.0` in all 5 projects |
| **DocumentationFile** | Duplicated in 3 src projects; uses `$(OutputPath)` which belongs in `.targets` |
| **PrintBuildInfo target** | Copy-pasted in all 3 src projects |
| **No Central Package Management** | Versions inline; **version drift detected** (e.g., `Logging.Abstractions` 8.0.0 vs 8.0.1, `xunit` 2.8.1 vs 2.7.1) |
| **Test boilerplate** | `IsPackable=false` and 4 identical test packages in both test projects |

Let me implement the fix — creating `Directory.Build.props`, `Directory.Build.targets`, `Directory.Packages.props`, and a `test/Directory.Build.props`, then slimming every `.csproj`.

  <!-- DocumentationFile depends on $(OutputPath), which is SDK-defined and not available in .props -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(IsTestProject)' != 'true'">
    <DocumentationFile>$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build" Condition="'$(IsTestProject)' != 'true'">
    <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
  </Target>

</Project>

<Project>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <!-- src packages -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" Version="8.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Swashbuckle.AspNetCore" Version="6.5.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="8.0.5" />
    <!-- test packages -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.10.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="NSubstitute" Version="5.1.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.8.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.1" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me implement all the improvements:

1. **Root `Directory.Build.props`** — centralize shared properties (Nullable, ImplicitUsings, TreatWarningsAsErrors, EnforceCodeStyleInBuild, Company, Authors)

2. **Root `Directory.Build.targets`** — move DocumentationFile (depends on OutputPath) and PrintBuildInfo target here

3. **Root `Directory.Packages.props`** — Central Package Management with all versions. Need to resolve version drift:
   - `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions`: 8.0.0 (Core, Api) vs 8.0.1 (Services) → use 8.0.1 (latest)
   - `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk`: 17.10.0 (Core.Tests) vs 17.9.0 (Api.Tests) → use 17.10.0
   - `xunit`: 2.8.1 (Core.Tests) vs 2.7.1 (Api.Tests) → use 2.8.1
   - `xunit.runner.visualstudio`: 2.8.1 (Core.Tests) vs 2.5.6 (Api.Tests) → use 2.8.1

4. **`src/Directory.Build.props`** — import parent and layer in source-specific settings

5. **`test/Directory.Build.props`** — import parent, set IsPackable=false, add shared test packages

6. **Simplify all .csproj files** — remove centralized properties, remove Version= from PackageReferences

Actually, I'm reconsidering whether I need a separate src/Directory.Build.props at all. The DocumentationFile and PrintBuildInfo target are the only src-specific things, and those belong in a targets file anyway. I could put them in a root Directory.Build.targets instead, conditionally applied only to src projects since test projects don't use DocumentationFile.

I have two approaches: create a src/Directory.Build.targets to keep src concerns isolated, or add conditions to the root targets file to exclude test projects. The first option is cleaner architecturally, though it means setting up the import chain properly with GetPathOfFileAbove.

Looking at the actual structure I need, the simplest path is to put shared properties in the root Directory.Build.props, then use conditional logic in root Directory.Build.targets for DocumentationFile and PrintBuildInfo based on IsTestProject. I'll add a test/Directory.Build.props for test-specific settings like IsPackable=false, and skip creating anything src-specific since there's nothing props-wise that's unique to src projects.

For DocumentationFile specifically, since it depends on SDK-defined properties like OutputPath, I'm setting it in the targets file with a condition that excludes test projects.

Now I'm ready to implement this. I'll create the root Directory.Build.props file with common properties like target framework, nullable reference types, implicit usings, and warning settings, then move on to the targets file.

Writing build configuration files... Defining the centralized package versions... Now I'm looking at the other project files in the solution—the Core library with logging abstractions and JSON serialization, and the Web API project that adds Swagger documentation and references the Core project.

Defining the Services and test project configurations...

data.content: The nested single quotes in the MSBuild condition need escaping. Let me fix the `test/Directory.Build.props`:
data.reasoningText:
The issue is with the single quotes inside the Condition string. MSBuild uses single quotes for string literals in conditions, so having single quotes inside the `GetPathOfFileAbove` call inside the `Exists()` inside the `Condition` attribute creates ambiguity. I need to use the `'` entity or restructure. Actually, the standard pattern uses `'` in the condition or stores the result in a property first.

Let me look at the file I created:

```xml
<Import Project="$([MSBuild]::GetPathOfFileAbove('Directory.Build.props', '$(MSBuildThisFileDirectory)../'))"
       Condition="Exists('$([MSBuild]::GetPathOfFileAbove('Directory.Build.props', '$(MSBuildThisFileDirectory)../'))')" />
```

The nested single quotes are the problem — the outer quotes for `Exists('...')` clash with the inner quotes for `GetPathOfFileAbove('...')`. I could either store the path in a property first to avoid the nesting, or rethink the condition approach entirely since a failed import will error anyway.

The cleaner solution is defining a property that holds the result of the path lookup, then using that property in a simpler condition check. This avoids the quote escaping issue and makes the logic more readable.

data.content:
Here's a summary of what was done and why:

### New files created (4)

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `Directory.Build.props` | 8 shared properties centralized (TFM, Nullable, etc.) |
| `Directory.Build.targets` | `DocumentationFile` + `PrintBuildInfo` target — placed here because they depend on `$(OutputPath)`, which isn't available in `.props` |
| `Directory.Packages.props` | Central Package Management — single source of truth for all 8 package versions |
| `test/Directory.Build.props` | `IsPackable=false` + 4 shared test package references, chains to root via `GetPathOfFileAbove` |

### Key improvements

- **~30 duplicated property lines removed** across 5 projects
- **Version drift fixed**: `Logging.Abstractions` (8.0.0→8.0.1), `xunit` (2.7.1→2.8.1), `xunit.runner.visualstudio` (2.5.6→2.8.1), `Test.Sdk` (17.9.0→17.10.0) — all unified to latest
- **Test projects reduced to ~5 lines** each (just the `ProjectReference`)
- **CS1591 suppressed** — pre-existing issue from `DocumentationFile` + `TreatWarningsAsErrors` without XML doc comments
